pricing.source: OpenAI API pricing, standard short-context rates, 2026-06-24

hardChecks.label: Citation source ids
hardChecks.expectation: Every cited source id exists in the source packet

hardChecks.label: Cost metadata
hardChecks.expectation: estimatedUsd is present when pricing is configured

hardChecks.label: Token metadata
hardChecks.expectation: Input, cached-input, and output token counts are coherent

hardChecks.label: Provider and model metadata
hardChecks.expectation: Provider, model, and prompt version are present

hardChecks.label: Judge input boundary
hardChecks.expectation: Judge prompt omits eval-only object keys and holdout tuning fields

hardChecks.label: Judge claim coverage
hardChecks.expectation: LLM judge returns exactly one judgment for every briefing claim

claimJudgments.claimText: Median review wait time dropped from 19h to 13h after adding reviewer rotation, but 90th percentile wait remains 46h because complex changes bottleneck on the same two senior reviewers.
claimJudgments.explanation: S1 states median wait improved from 19 to 13 hours after reviewer rotation and that p90 remains 46 hours because complex changes often wait for the same two senior reviewers.

claimJudgments.claimText: Tail latency is driven mainly by cross-service/shared-service changes waiting on scarce, specific owners; docs and config changes moved quickly after rotation.
claimJudgments.explanation: S1 says documentation/configuration reviews moved quickly after rotation, while shared-service/cross-service changes waited on scarce reviewers and tail latency should be treated as an ownership-routing problem.

claimJudgments.claimText: Reviewers are positive about auto-assignment for routine changes but distrust bot approvals when tests are flaky or ownership is unclear; they want explainable risk labels (ownership metadata, test health, file categories) and the ability to challenge a fast-track label before merge.
claimJudgments.explanation: S2 directly supports reviewer support for auto-assignment, skepticism of bot approvals under flaky tests/unclear ownership, desired risk-label explanations, and challengeability before merge.

claimJudgments.claimText: Post-merge data shows no measurable defect increase for low-risk docs/config changes, but incidents occurred when cross-service changes were fast-tracked without the owning team; leadership wants different treatment for low-risk vs cross-boundary changes and a distinction between queue organization and approval authority.
claimJudgments.explanation: S3 states no measurable defect increase for low-risk docs/config changes, two incidents from fast-tracked cross-service changes without the owning team, and the review lead’s requested distinctions.

claimJudgments.claimText: Proposed tooling can suggest reviewers, group low-risk changes, and surface missing ownership metadata, but cannot verify semantic ownership and cannot override branch protection.
claimJudgments.explanation: S4 says the helper can suggest reviewers, group/cluster low-risk changes, surface missing ownership data, cannot verify semantic ownership, and cannot override branch protection.

recommendationJudgment.explanation: The recommendation is well aligned with the packet: it targets queue time through ownership routing for tail latency, limits fast-track to low-risk docs/config with healthy tests, preserves required approvals, uses automation for routing rather than approval, adds explainable risk labels and challengeability, and prioritizes stale shared-service ownership metadata. These points are supported across S1-S6 and appropriately avoid recommending bot approval or bypassing branch protection. Minor operational details, such as exact challenge-window mechanics or test-health thresholds, are framed as open questions rather than asserted facts.

citationSupport.note: S1 appears in fully supported claim-level judgments.
S2 appears in fully supported claim-level judgments.

S4 appears in fully supported claim-level judgments.

notes: Hybrid evaluator output: deterministic hard checks plus structured LLM judge evidence. Manual spot checks are still required before claiming product improvement.
